elements.sent-2411-001: Recent advances in multimodal Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown great success in understanding multimodal contents.
elements.sent-2411-002: For video understanding tasks, training-based video LLMs are difficult to build due to the scarcity of high-quality, curated video-text paired data.
elements.sent-2411-003: In contrast, paired image-text data are much easier to obtain, and there is substantial similarity between images and videos.
elements.sent-2411-004: Consequently, extending image LLMs for video understanding tasks presents an appealing alternative.
elements.sent-2411-005: Developing effective strategies for compressing visual tokens from multiple frames is a promising way to leverage the powerful pre-trained image LLM.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this work, we explore the limitations of the existing compression strategies for building a training-free video LLM.
elements.sent-2411-007: The findings lead to our method TS-LLaVA, which constructs visual tokens through a Thumbnail-and-Sampling strategy.
elements.sent-2411-008: Given a video, we select few equidistant frames from all input frames to construct a Thumbnail image as a detailed visual cue, complemented by Sampled visual tokens from all input frames.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our method establishes the new state-of-the-art performance among training-free video LLMs on various benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-010: Notably, our 34B model outperforms GPT-4V on the MVBench benchmark, and achieves performance comparable to the 72B training-based video LLM, Video-LLaMA2, on the challenging MLVU benchmark.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/teaser.pdf} \caption{Illustration of training-free video LLM. Vision Tower: vision encoder and projection module in image LLM. } \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-012: Thanks to the breakthroughs in foundation models like CLIP~\cite{pmlr-v139-radford21a and Large Language Models (LLMs)~\cite{touvron2023llama, bai2023qwen, jiang2024mixtralexperts, vicuna2023 , image LLMs~\cite{zhang2024llamaadapter, Qwen-VL, NEURIPS2023_llava, Liu_2024_CVPR have shown great success in comprehending visual contents and generating textual responses based on user prompts.}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Image LLMs are often trained with paired image-text data to bridge the representation spaces between the vision encoder and the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-014: Although trained in a similar way, video LLMs~\cite{maaz-etal-2024-video, lin2023videollava, xu2024pllava, li2024llamavid require far more resources and a larger volume of paired video-text data due to the increased complexity of video content.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Unfortunately, curated large-scale video-text data is hard to obtain.
elements.sent-2411-016: Since images are easier to acquire and share similar features with videos, extending image LLMs for video understanding presents a promising approach.
elements.sent-2411-017: We illustrate the training-free video LLM in~\cref{fig:teaser .}
elements.sent-2411-018: To bypass the high computational cost in training-based video LLM, several methods have been proposed that use pre-trained image LLMs to build training-free video LLMs~\cite{wu2024freevaofflinemllmtrainingfree, xu2024slowfastllavastrongtrainingfreebaseline, kim2024imagegridworthvideo .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Given an image LLM, the vision tower directly encodes the spatial information of each frame, while the autoregressive nature of the LLM captures the temporal information within the input sequence to some extent.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, the number of visual tokens\footnote{i.e. elements of the encoded visual features, used as input for the LLM.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Consequently, a key design aspect of training-free video LLM is the visual token compression strategy, which enables image LLMs to efficiently handle video input.
elements.sent-2411-022: To better understand the working mechanisms of training-free video LLM, we begin by evaluating the commonly used compression strategies.
elements.sent-2411-023: Insights gained from examining these visual token compression strategies led to the development of our method, TS-LLaVA.
elements.sent-2411-024: TS-LLaVA adopts a hybrid manner to compress visual tokens.
elements.sent-2411-025: Given $N$ input frames, we select $N_T$ ($N_T \ll N$) frames to construct a Thumbnail image that serves as a summary of the video.
elements.sent-2411-026: We show that using this thumbnail image alone enables the model to comprehend certain aspects of the video contents, though it still leaves some nuances unaddressed.
elements.sent-2411-027: To address this, we complement the thumbnail image by incorporating Sampled visual tokens from all frames, which leads to a deeper and more complete grasp of the video content.
elements.sent-2411-028: Together, the Thumbnail-and-Sampling strategy constitutes the core of our TS-LLaVA.
elements.sent-2411-029: We evaluate our method on various video understanding benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-030: Experimental results show that TS-LLaVA outperforms the previous state-of-the-art (SOTA) training-free video LLMs by a large margin.
elements.sent-2411-031: Notably, our 34B model surpasses GPT-4V~\cite{gpt4v on the MVBench benchmark~\cite{Li_2024_CVPR , and achieves performance comparable to the 72B training-based video LLM Video-LLaMA2~\cite{cheng2024videollama2advancingspatialtemporal on the challenging MLVU benchmark~\cite{zhou2024mlvu .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: We conduct comprehensive ablation studies to assess the design choices of TS-LLaVA.
elements.sent-2411-033: Together with the study on compression strategies, we hope to provide insights for future works.
elements.sent-2411-034: In summary, our main contributions include:.
elements.sent-2411-035: We conduct a thorough evaluation of various compression strategies for training-free video LLMs, offering insights to guide future research in developing video LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-036: Based on our findings, we propose the Thumbnail-and-Sampling compression strategy, which shows clear advantages over existing approaches.
elements.sent-2411-037: Leveraging the Thumbnail-and-Sampling strategy, we develop TS-LLaVA, establishing a new SOTA among training-free video LLMs while maintaining high token efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-038: Image LLMs aim to bridge the representation space between the vision encoder~\cite{pmlr-v139-radford21a and LLM~\cite{touvron2023llama, bai2023qwen, jiang2024mixtralexperts, vicuna2023 using image-text data.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The BLIP family, including BLIP-2~\cite{icml_blip2 and InstructBLIP~\cite{dai2023instructblip , employs a Querying Transformer (QFormer) to connect vision and language modalities via learnable queries in cross-attention modules.}}

elements.sent-2411-042: The LLaVA family, in contrast, uses an MLP for connecting the vision encoder and LLM.

elements.sent-2411-055: Training-free video LLMs extend image LLMs for video understanding without requiring additional fine-tuning on video data.
elements.sent-2411-056: As a pioneering approach, IG-VLM~\cite{kim2024imagegridworthvideo constructs a grid-view image from video frames, which is then fed directly into a frozen image LLM with a specially designed prompt.}

elements.sent-2411-058: FreeVA~\cite{wu2024freevaofflinemllmtrainingfree explores various temporal aggregation methods, but similarly uses a limited number of frames.}
elements.sent-2411-059: The current state-of-the-art SF-LLaVA~\cite{xu2024slowfastllavastrongtrainingfreebaseline adopts the slow-fast design, which is proven to be effective in action recognition~\cite{Feichtenhofer_2019_ICCV, xiao2020audiovisualslowfastnetworksvideo , and in LITA, as mentioned earlier.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: SF-LLaVA designs a slow pathway compressing fewer frames, and a fast pathway heavily compressing more frames.
elements.sent-2411-061: While both SF-LLaVA and our method use a two-stream design, our Thumbnail-and-Sampling strategy leads to significantly better performance on various video understanding benchmarks, while maintaining a better token efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-063: To equip the baseline image LLM with the video understanding abilities, various visual token compression strategies are applied.
elements.sent-2411-064: They allow incorporating multiple frames from the videos.
elements.sent-2411-065: Including commonly used methods, we evaluate five types of compression strategies as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-067: Pooling: Applying spatial average pooling to visual tokens.\footnote{Different pooling operations are compared in the Appendix.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Grid: Creating a single grid-view image composed by $N_T$ frames to represent a video.
elements.sent-2411-069: Grids: For $N=k\times N_T$ frames, generating $k$ grid-view images, each composed of $N_T$ frames.
elements.sent-2411-070: Sampling: Applying uniform sampling to visual tokens.
elements.sent-2411-071: Specifically, Concat is one of the core ideas of the training-free video LLM FreeVA~\cite{wu2024freevaofflinemllmtrainingfree .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Pooling is a widely used token compression method for video LLMs~\cite{li2024llamavid, cheng2024videollama2advancingspatialtemporal, huang2024litalanguageinstructedtemporallocalization, maaz-etal-2024-video, xu2024pllava, zhang2024longcontexttransferlanguage , including training-free methods~\cite{wu2024freevaofflinemllmtrainingfree, xu2024slowfastllavastrongtrainingfreebaseline .}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Grid, as proposed by IG-VLM~\cite{kim2024imagegridworthvideo , aims to transform video frames into a grid image for better interaction with image LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-074: We extend this to Grids by composing multiple grid-view images.
elements.sent-2411-075: Finally, we also evaluate Sampling as a less explored compression strategy for video LLMs.

elements.sent-2411-084: The simplest approach to adapting an image LLM for video understanding is to concatenate frames (Concat).
elements.sent-2411-085: While Concat avoids compressing visual tokens, it is limited by the number of frames it can process.
elements.sent-2411-086: Concat is considered as the baseline method, which shows how powerful is the image LLM for video understanding tasks without any modification.

elements.sent-2411-089: Composing grid-view images (Grid, Grids) yields promising results.
elements.sent-2411-090: Grid can be seen as using a thumbnail image to represent the video.
elements.sent-2411-091: Even with only 1/4 of the tokens used by other methods, Grid achieves satisfactory performance, allowing for reasonable video comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-092: However, due to its limited number of frames and visual tokens, Grid is ultimately outperformed by other compression strategies.

elements.sent-2411-104: In this section, we introduce TS-LLaVA and its core idea, the Thumbnail-and-Sampling strategy.
elements.sent-2411-105: The experiment in~\cref{sec:comparing shows that both Grids and Sampling offer superior video understanding capabilities compared to other strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-106: However, due to their respective designs, Grids is limited to low-resolution frames; while Sampling risks losing details from unsampled tokens.
elements.sent-2411-107: How to overcome the shortcomings of these two compression strategies? Recall that Grid, a simplified version of Grids, performs well on reasoning tasks but is constrained by the limited number of frames and visual tokens it can process.
elements.sent-2411-108: To leverage the strengths of both approaches, we propose an intuitive method to combine their benefits, as illustrated in~\cref{fig:method .}
elements.sent-2411-109: We select few equidistant frames from all input frames to create a grid-view image (Grid), which serves as the thumbnail image for the video.
elements.sent-2411-110: The missing information from this thumbnail is then complemented by sampling visual tokens from all input frames (Sampling) at the original resolution.
elements.sent-2411-111: This approach creates a more versatile compression strategy, effectively combining the strengths of both Grids and Sampling.
elements.sent-2411-112: Specifically, given an input video, we first uniformly sample $N$ frames from the video.
elements.sent-2411-113: These frames are further used for video understanding with a given image LLM.
elements.sent-2411-114: To construct Thumbnail image tokens, we further select $N_T$ equidistant frames from the initially obtained $N$ frames, where $N_T \ll N$ and $N_T \equiv 0 \pmod{2}$.
elements.sent-2411-115: We create the thumbnail image by arranging these frames into a single grid-view image, $I_T$, that accommodates all selected $N_T$ frames.
elements.sent-2411-116: To construct Sampled image tokens, all $N$ frames are used.
elements.sent-2411-117: We first extract visual features frame by frame, resulting in features $F\in \mathbb{R ^{N\times V}$.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Given a pre-defined maximum number of visual tokens $M$, we apply: \begin{equation}F\in \mathbb{R}^{N\times V} \xrightarrow{\text{Sample}} F_S \in \mathbb{R}^{M-V}\end{equation} where Sample denotes uniform sampling, and $V < M <$ context length of LLM.
elements.sent-2411-119: Finally, we obtain the visual tokens by concatenating $F_S$ and $F_T$ as $[F_S, F_T]$, which is further passed to LLM to interact with encoded textual inputs for video understanding.
elements.sent-2411-120: To demonstrate the advantages of our Thumbnail-and-Sampling strategy, we conduct experiments on Video-MME under the same setting as in~\cref{sec:comparing .}
elements.sent-2411-121: From the 16 input frames, we select 4 ($\dag$) or 6 ($\ddag$) equidistant frames to compose the thumbnail image, resulting in 576 encoded visual tokens.
elements.sent-2411-122: All 16 input frames are encoded to extract the sampled visual tokens.
elements.sent-2411-123: We use 2304 visual tokens in total.
elements.sent-2411-124: The results are presented in~\cref{tab:ours_videomme , where our method achieves the best overall performance.}
elements.sent-2411-125: Our compression strategy brings notable improvements on both short and long videos, while maintaining the strong performance of Sampling or Grid/Grids on medium-length videos.
elements.sent-2411-126: Compared to Grids, Sampling lags on spatial reasoning, while it excels on action reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-127: Our strategy improves the performance on these two tasks by a large margin, indicating that our strategy integrates the advantages of Grid and Sampling.

elements.sent-2411-166: Multitask Benchmarks To comprehensively evaluate the capabilities of our TS-LLaVA, we further conduct experiments on two challenging multitask video understanding benchmarks, namely MVBench and MLVU.
elements.sent-2411-167: MVBench We present the results in~\cref{tab:mvbench .}
elements.sent-2411-168: Among the training-free methods, our 34B model outperforms the proprietary model GPT-4V by a large margin, in both average accuracy and across most sub-tasks.
elements.sent-2411-169: Even our 7B model surpasses GPT-4V in average accuracy, showing the strong understanding capability and potential of our method.
elements.sent-2411-170: When comparing to the training-based video LLM, we focus on PLLaVA~\cite{xu2024pllava .}
elements.sent-2411-171: PLLaVA uses the same image LLM backbone as our model but is further trained on video data.
elements.sent-2411-172: In over half of the sub-tasks, our TS-LLaVA manages to obtain comparable or better performance than PLLaVA.
elements.sent-2411-173: However, there are still tasks where performance can be improved: (1) On some action centric tasks, TS-LLaVA delivers satisfactory results (AC and AP).
elements.sent-2411-174: However, it struggles with other action-centric tasks (e.g., AA, AL, and AS).
elements.sent-2411-175: Given the nature of these task types, TS-LLaVA's lower performance is unsurprising.
elements.sent-2411-176: All of these tasks involve data types not seen during image LLM training, such as actions and moving objects.
elements.sent-2411-177: Without specific training on these data types, bridging the gap between our training-free approach and training-based methods remains challenging.
elements.sent-2411-178: MLVU The results are presented in~\cref{tab:mlvu .}
elements.sent-2411-179: Our method significantly outperforms the training-free counterpart, establishing the SOTA results across all sub-tasks.
elements.sent-2411-180: In comparison with training-based video LLM, we take a challenging opponent, namely a 72B Video-LLaMA2.
elements.sent-2411-181: Remarkably, TS-LLaVA-34B achieves comparable or even superior performance to Video-LLaMA2-72B on more than half of the sub-tasks, despite using a much smaller LLM.
elements.sent-2411-182: For task types where TS-LLaVA lags behind, we observe a similar pattern as in MVBench.
elements.sent-2411-183: Tasks involving data types less commonly encountered during image LLM training, such as action order, pose greater challenges for our training-free method TS-LLaVA.
elements.sent-2411-184: Overall Our TS-LLaVA demonstrates promising results on two challenging multitask benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-185: However, due to inherent limitations of image LLMs, certain tasks remain difficult to perform.

We compare 7B models here.} \label{fig:token_num} \end{subfigure} \caption{Design choices of TS-LLaVA. In (a), (b) (c), IntentQA shows similar pattern as NExT-QA, please refer to the Appendix.}
elements.sent-2411-186: We dive deeper into the design choices of TS-LLaVA in this section.
elements.sent-2411-187: More results can be found in the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-188: Number of frames We vary the maximum number of input frames used in TS-LLaVA, and present the results in~\cref{fig:frame_num .}
elements.sent-2411-189: Since the total number of visual tokens remains constant at 3456, using fewer frames results in a lower compression rate.
elements.sent-2411-190: For datasets like NExT-QA, which do not emphasize long-term video understanding, the reduced compression rate effectively compensates for any missing information from fewer frames.
elements.sent-2411-191: In contrast, for EgoSchema, which specifically targets long-term understanding, using more frames proves beneficial.
elements.sent-2411-192: One interesting observation is that the 7B and 34B models behave differently to reduced frame counts.
elements.sent-2411-193: The performance of the 34B model keeps increasing as we increase the number of frames, indicating it can handle more information as we increase frame numbers.
elements.sent-2411-194: How many frames per thumbnail image? We conduct experiments with varying numbers of frames per thumbnail image.
elements.sent-2411-196: Since the resolution of each image is fixed at 336$\times$336 for the image encoder, including more frames in the thumbnail image means lower resolution for each frame.
elements.sent-2411-197: The results show that changing the number of frames does not affect the performance on NExT-QA and IntentQA significantly.
elements.sent-2411-198: While for EgoSchema, which requires better temporal understanding and involves longer videos, is more sensitive to the number of frames per thumbnail image.
elements.sent-2411-199: Using 6 frames per thumbnail image shows clear benefit over the counterparts, by providing enough temporal cues and not losing too much details due to reduced resolution.
elements.sent-2411-200: How many thumbnail images? To study the effect of the number thumbnail images on the performance, we report the results of using 1, 2 and 3 thumbnail images in our method in~\cref{fig:grid_num .}
elements.sent-2411-201: Since each thumbnail image corresponds to 576 visual tokens in our method, increasing the number of thumbnail images leads to a higher compression rate for the sampled visual tokens.
elements.sent-2411-202: With 50 input frames, the compression rate is $\frac{50\times 576 {3456-576\times k}$, with $k$ being the number of thumbnail images.}
elements.sent-2411-203: The increased compression rate degrades the performance on EgoSchema significantly, which cannot be mitigated by the added thumbnail image tokens.
elements.sent-2411-204: It indicates that for tasks that require a temporal understanding, less compressed sampled visual tokens are preferred.
elements.sent-2411-205: Token efficiency Image LLMs are not trained on videos involving multiple frames, making token efficiency a critical factor for training-free video LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-206: Compared to the previous SOTA training-free video LLM SF-LLaVA, we gradually decrease the number of visual tokens in TS-LLaVA, and present the results in~\cref{fig:token_num .}
elements.sent-2411-207: Our TS-LLaVA consistently outperforms SF-LLaVA, even with roughly 60\% of the number of visual tokens used by SF-LLaVA (2304 vs 3680).
elements.sent-2411-208: Our Thumbnail-and-Sampling strategy can better compress the visual tokens than the slow-fast operation used by SF-LLaVA.
elements.sent-2411-210: More discussions on SOTA methods Our Thumbnail-and-Sampling strategy shares similarities to the widely used slow-fast operations in the video understanding domain~\cite{Feichtenhofer_2019_ICCV, xiao2020audiovisualslowfastnetworksvideo, huang2024litalanguageinstructedtemporallocalization, xu2024slowfastllavastrongtrainingfreebaseline , including the previous SOTA training-free video LLM, SF-LLaVA.}
elements.sent-2411-211: Both adopt a two-stream design for processing video frames, the major difference between our compression strategy and slow-fast operation in SF-LLaVA lies in how frames are handled:.
elements.sent-2411-212: SF-LLaVA: Slow pathway keeps fewer frames with a lower frame rate, but with a lower compression rate.
elements.sent-2411-213: Fast pathway keeps more frames at a higher frame rate, with higher compression rate.
elements.sent-2411-214: TS-LLaVA: Thumbnail pathway uses fewer frames in lower resolution.
elements.sent-2411-215: Sampling pathway includes more frames at a higher resolution, but only retains information from sampled tokens.
elements.sent-2411-216: In SF-LLaVA, most of the token budget (2880 out of 3680 tokens) is allocated to the slow pathway, resulting in a high compression rate of 36 in the fast pathway with 50 frames.
elements.sent-2411-217: Our method preserves more information in the Sampling pathway, where 50 frames use 2880 tokens, yielding a compression rate of 10.
elements.sent-2411-218: We capture long-term dependencies effectively due to less compressed tokens, while maintaining better token efficiency, as validated in~\cref{fig:token_num .}
elements.sent-2411-219: Consequently, our method promotes token efficiency when representing the video content, which is an important advantage.
elements.sent-2411-220: Limitations Though establishing the SOTA performance among all training-free video LLMs, our primary focus is on visual token compression strategies rather than prompt design.
elements.sent-2411-221: Previous works show the prompt can affect the performance to some extent~\cite{kim2024imagegridworthvideo , which is not clearly observed in our preliminary experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-222: The slightly reduced resolution from the Thumbnail image can be mitigated by existing high-resolution operations in image LLMs~\cite{Liu_2024_CVPR , which feeds image patch features to the LLM.}
elements.sent-2411-223: There is also potential in exploring combining vision encoders through feature routing~\cite{tingyu24 .}
elements.sent-2411-224: We leave the above for future works.
elements.sent-2411-225: In this work, we thoroughly evaluate the advantages and limitations of various visual token compression strategies for training-free video LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-226: Our findings lead to the development of TS-LLaVA, a training-free video LLM that utilizes the newly proposed Thumbnail-and-Sampling compression strategy.
elements.sent-2411-227: This strategy provides both a summarized view of the video (Thumbnail) and a detailed view of the long-term temporal relations (Sampling).
elements.sent-2411-228: As a simple but strong baseline, TS-LLaVA establishes the new state-of-the-art among training-free video LLMs, and surpasses several strong training-based video LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-229: Our extensive experiments offer valuable insights for future works on building video LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-230: To construct the thumbnail image, we arrange the selected frames in a 2-column by 3-row grid.
elements.sent-2411-231: Each frame is resized accordingly to fit within the resulting 336$\times$336 thumbnail image.
elements.sent-2411-232: MVBench Details We report the detailed classification of each sub-task included in MVBench:.
elements.sent-2411-233: Action: Action Sequence (AS), Action Antonym (AA), Action Prediction (AP), Unexpected Action (UA) and Fine-grained Action (FA).
elements.sent-2411-234: Object: Object Shuffle (OS), Object Existence (OE) and Object Interaction (OI).
elements.sent-2411-235: Position: Moving Direction (MD) and Action Localization (AL).
elements.sent-2411-236: Count: Action Count (AC) and Moving Count (MC).
elements.sent-2411-239: Attribute: State Change (SC) and Moving Attribute (MA).
elements.sent-2411-241: Cognition: Episodic Reasoning (ER), Egocentric Navigation (EN) and Counterfactual Inference (CI).

elements.sent-2411-245: Finally, we conclude this section with more results on design choices of TS-LLaVA.

elements.sent-2411-251: Evaluation protocol Following the common practice, we use GPT-assisted evaluation for Open-Ended VideoQA and Text Generation benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-252: The evaluation assesses the accuracy of the response (in terms of true or false) and the quality of the generated text (score ranging from 0 to 5).
elements.sent-2411-253: As pointed in~\cite{wu2024freevaofflinemllmtrainingfree, xu2024slowfastllavastrongtrainingfreebaseline , different GPT versions have significant impacts on the evaluation results.}
elements.sent-2411-254: To keep a fair comparison, we adopt GPT-3.5-Turbo-0125 as in~\cite{wu2024freevaofflinemllmtrainingfree, xu2024slowfastllavastrongtrainingfreebaseline .}

elements.sent-2411-256: Our method outperforms the previous SOTA SF-LLaVA on majority of the tasks evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-257: We consider the evaluation protocol for Open-Ended VideoQA and video-based Text Generation not as reliable as Multiple Choice VideoQA.
